h2.note: transcript-level proxy for the per-turn H2 (§4.3); needs a real (non-mock) full-N run to mean anything

scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner mechanically applies the food-to-eater rule to each subsequent arrow without internalizing the underlying concept.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: `hawk -> rabbit` says energy in hawk moves into rabbit; for this feeding, name the food, name the eater, then flip the arrow if the food label comes second.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor shifts from stating an abstract rule to providing a concrete semantic translation of the arrow to expose the logical absurdity of the learner's error.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: There is no pivot learner turn to which the tutor could contingently adapt.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: There is no pivot learner turn to which the tutor could contingently adapt.

tutor_strategy_reversal.evidence: `hawk -> rabbit` says energy in hawk moves into rabbit; for this feeding, name the food, name the eater, then flip the arrow if the food label comes second.
tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: The tutor shifts from stating an abstract rule to providing a concrete semantic translation of the arrow to expose the logical absurdity of the learner's error.

tutor_adaptive_mechanism.evidence: `hawk -> rabbit` says energy in hawk moves into rabbit; for this feeding, name the food, name the eater, then flip the arrow if the food label comes second.
tutor_adaptive_mechanism.justification: The tutor shifts from stating an abstract rule to providing a concrete semantic translation of the arrow to expose the logical absurdity of the learner's error.

scored.recoheredEarlier: I can put 2 in both of these, but then it looks like I missed the point of having two labels.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner would simply accept the tutor's explanation and move on to the next problem without further pushback.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: Keep the complaint. The bare 2 is not enough. With the labels covered, say which 2 compares 3 to 6 and which 2 compares 12 to 24.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor invents an object-work mechanism by physically covering the labels to validate the learner's persistent complaint and prove why the labels are necessary.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: Now the worksheet has to carry that difference: side scale compares one side length. Perimeter scale compares the whole border total.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: The tutor adapts to the learner's realization about the labels by explicitly framing the worksheet itself as the carrier of the semantic difference.

learner_self_reframe.evidence: I can put 2 in both of these, but then it looks like I missed the point of having two labels.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.evidence: Now the worksheet has to carry that difference: side scale compares one side length. Perimeter scale compares the whole border total.
tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: The tutor adapts to the learner's realization about the labels by explicitly framing the worksheet itself as the carrier of the semantic difference.

tutor_strategy_reversal.evidence: Keep the complaint. The bare 2 is not enough. With the labels covered, say which 2 compares 3 to 6 and which 2 compares 12 to 24.
tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: The tutor invents an object-work mechanism by physically covering the labels to validate the learner's persistent complaint and prove why the labels are necessary.

tutor_adaptive_mechanism.evidence: Keep the complaint. The bare 2 is not enough. With the labels covered, say which 2 compares 3 to 6 and which 2 compares 12 to 24.
tutor_adaptive_mechanism.justification: The tutor invents an object-work mechanism by physically covering the labels to validate the learner's persistent complaint and prove why the labels are necessary.

scored.recoheredEarlier: I still want to call it double.
scored.statedInsightEvidence: Fine, the side is double, but the grid is not letting me call the area double.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner was heading toward simply memorizing the area formula while remaining confused about why the side being double doesn't make the area double.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: If the side factor were \(3\), what area factor would the same grid logic force?
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor shifts the cognitive load from correcting the immediate error to applying the newly formed conceptual rule to a novel parameter.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: Keep the dislike; it is not in charge of the count.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: The tutor directly addresses the learner's expressed emotional friction about being wrong, integrating it into the pedagogical frame before moving to a transfer question.

learner_self_reframe.evidence: I still want to call it double.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.evidence: Keep the dislike; it is not in charge of the count.
tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: The tutor directly addresses the learner's expressed emotional friction about being wrong, integrating it into the pedagogical frame before moving to a transfer question.

tutor_strategy_reversal.evidence: If the side factor were \(3\), what area factor would the same grid logic force?
tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: The tutor shifts the cognitive load from correcting the immediate error to applying the newly formed conceptual rule to a novel parameter.

tutor_adaptive_mechanism.evidence: If the side factor were \(3\), what area factor would the same grid logic force?
tutor_adaptive_mechanism.justification: The tutor shifts the cognitive load from correcting the immediate error to applying the newly formed conceptual rule to a novel parameter.
